sentences.text: Good morning.
I was born in Ostrozec in 1925, December-- 15 of December.
The name, Pola, P-O-L-A.
Shteinshneid, S-H-T-E-I-N-S-H-N-E-I-D.
Ostrozec.
This is near Lutsk-- 16 kilometers from Lutsk.
In Poland.
This was a shtetl, about 1,000 families-- maybe less, maybe more.
And we had three temples.
And we had a school-- a public school.
And we had private teachers, which they will teach Hebrew.
And-- and privately, all kinds of religious things.
None.
In the shtetl, there was none not Jewish.
Around was-- the village, [INAUDIBLE] Ostrozec, and there was mostly Ukrainian, and some Polish teachers, or from the post office, was living there.
But mostly Ukrainians.
No, they used to come-- they didn't have business together.
They used to come to buy something in the shtetl.
Everything they used to buy, to come to the Jewish people, Jewish stores.
It was-- for instance, it was this super-- not supermarket-- grocery stores.
And was stores-- materials for clothing.
And so nothing more.
David.
David Shteinshneid.
My father was this forest-- used to buy forest, and to make out all kind of material from the forest, from the tress.
For instance, for the-- for the railroad-- how you say--
No, not the-- the wood.
And for furniture, for eating-- everything had he sell [INAUDIBLE].
And for building, also.
And then we had a farm, And he used to buy-- we had a Count Liderhowsky-- a Polish count.
The count was Polish, a very good man.
He used-- every winter, my father used to go, and he used to donate some wood for the poor people.
Potatoes he used to give, and some flour for the poor people-- twice a year, and for Easter.
Very poor people.
For instance, there was these widows, which their husbands died.
There was no social security.
Was no help for nobody.
My mother used to collect bread, sugar, or some other things, and used to put down by the door that she shouldn't even know where was given.
She was ashamed to take, you know?
And we used-- when was a wedding or a bar mitzvah, or a child was born, I helped my mother to make such a-- to pins, and you give a donation, [NON-ENGLISH].
And for these donations, they went for [INAUDIBLE], and then some to help out people, poor people.
This was their [INAUDIBLE].
This was my stepmother.
But this was my-- my mother's name was Milka, my stepmother's.
But--
Milka Shteinshneid-- from home, Milka Katz, I think, Katz.
Yes.
I was a year old when she came.
Chaya [?
Goula. ?] Chaya Goula.
Yeah, Chaya Goula.
But they used to call her Goula.
Milamed ?] [INAUDIBLE].
She was-- she died when my birth.
I was the fifth.
Yes.
And after a year, he remarried.
The oldest was Rivka.
My brother was Chaim.
My sister was Minde-- Mindel.
And my fourth-- fourth sibling was Sarah.
She died when she was 7 years old.
And me.
And then was born by my stepmother-- she couldn't have children.
She was the-- her husband divorced her after 10 years because she couldn't have.
And that time, they didn't know the reason.
Always was the fault of the woman.
He divorced her.
My father didn't want more children.
Then he married her.
She was a very nice-looking woman.
And to the year, she had a child.
And this was my brother.
We grew up together, I with him.
We were very, very close.
Only with them, I called her mother, and they called her [NON-ENGLISH]-- aunt.
And I didn't know that she's not my mother until 8 years I was old.
Eli.
Eli.
My middle sister, Mindel, she was very jealous of me.
I was very rotten spoiled.
And I used to beat her sometime up, you know, and my older sister, there were big differences.
I think the oldest was maybe 13 years older.
I don't know.
No, 12 years.
Until I know that she was already engaged.
And my brother also was much older.
This sister also.
And then the other sister, and my mother was-- heart trouble, she had.
The doctor told her she shouldn't have no children anymore.
She'll die.
She was very religious, and she told him, I don't want no abortion to make.
And I'll have what God will give.
Then I was alive and she died.
Then she-- I made too much trouble, and she say, you killed my mother.
But I was 8 years old.
And I think, why?
No, every little thing, I say, I have a mother.
I'll tell my mother.
I'll tell my mother.
And you know, my mother used to stick with me, because you know, she didn't take part between us.
We used to beat-- the brother and me, I used to beat him up.
I was older, two years older.
But she didn't want to [? mix in. ?] And then we made up and we kissed.
Then she say, you killed my mother.
I said, why, why?
Because when you-- she had you, and you killed her.
Then I wept.
I start to cry, and everything.
All year, I was very, very unhappy.
And since then, I start, you know, to-- this was already not a mother, then a stepmother.
[INAUDIBLE].
Before she was very good.
I was very happy.
And since then, I said oh, with my mother, and I missed my mother.
I didn't have no picture, nothing.
They hide it from me, everything.
I shouldn't know.
I don't know my mother, even from a picture.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
No, mother, I called her.
Then after one year, you know I saw, and I understand.
And I was, you know, but still-- not this, what was.
At night, I didn't sleep.
I want to see my mother, at least in sleep.
And then-- but we-- when Hitler came, we--
We ran away together, I and my mother and the brother.
Yes.
Yes, I remember very good, going to school.
It was a big anti-Semitism.
They're all-- the Ukrainian and the Polish, saying [NON-ENGLISH]-- Jews to Palestine.
Go back to Palestine.
They used to beat us up.
And it was very bad.
But we didn't have no choice.
We needed to go.
Some teachers, you know, protect us, and some, you know, like they didn't hear or didn't see, you know.
All the subjects, you know.
Polish was the main subject.
Polish, geography-- not nature--
No, mathematics were very, very important.
And then gymnastics very, also.
And no--
Needlepoint.
And also this drawing.
No, we had a lot.
This is true.
We had a lot more to do.
Every day before we went to gymnastic, we made-- and once a day, many, many gymnastic put out the nerves, the energy.
My favorite subject was this [NON-ENGLISH], and this history.
History was my favorite.
And the languages.
No-- Ukrainian, I learned at school.
Hebrew, Yiddish, and that's all.
Yes.
Yiddish.
Yiddish.
Only Yiddish.
A nice holiday-- my father, you know, because he was, you know-- in that time, he was the richest man in our shtetl.
He had the farm, and we had this forest business, and the lumber business.
And our shul was down.
Then he built it up, renovated it.
And he was like [INAUDIBLE] gabbi in shul.
Olyka-- Olyka Shul.
Olyka because Olyka had a rebbe.
They called him Olyka Rebbe like Lubavitcher rebbe, because he was from Lvov.
Olyka Rebbe-- my father was Olyka Rebbe Hasid.
And the shul was Olyka Rebbe's name.
And he used to come to the shtetl to visit the Hasidim.
He used to stay by us.
He gave me my [INAUDIBLE], and a brucha, a blessing.
I was very sick.
He gave me a blessing.
I was usually a very sick child-- wild, spoiled, and sick-- a very sick child.
And he told, don't worry.
She'll grow.
She'll be well, she'll be-- God will help her.
She'll overcome.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then he used to go back to Olyka.
In Olyka, I had my sister married to Olyka.
I had already a married sister with two children.
The sister's name was Rivka.
My brother-in-law's was Chema.
The children was [?
Manya, ?] Miriam, and the boy was Chaim.
Also they called him [?
Munia. ?]
Family name-- Dreishbun.
And--
The same like from Lutsk.
Like 15, 15, 16 kilometers.
The same.
We used to go very often.
Then my brother--
By-- with horses.
We had-- how you say-- [INAUDIBLE]
A carriage and horses.
Yes.
And they came every vacation, every summer, they came out on the farm.
And they came to us, like [INAUDIBLE].
And then I had a brother married to [INAUDIBLE] Olyka.
And he had a wife, Hannah Glickman from [INAUDIBLE], and two children, a son, Berela, and a daughter, Ruchela.
And they used to come.
I used to go there very often.
At home was Mindel, me, and my brother, because the other died.
Sarah.
Yes.
This was before Passover.
We ate together.
We ate supper, and she told us that she has stomachaches.
And we had a Dr.
Romanov.
She told us-- she was a friend with my father, with my mother, and she told us secretly that she is from the family from the Czar Romanov.
The doctor.
And she wanted to teach me Russian.
I didn't want.
She spoke Russian.
I spoke Polish.
And she say, one day, maybe you need to use the Russian language.
And then when the Russians came, she said, you see?
I told you.
No, I didn't know Russian, not at all.
I didn't like the Russian language.
I don't know why.
How far?
Very near.
It was a small shtetl.
Seven before the Russians came.
Yes, of course.
This was-- one moment.
I think also June.
No-- one moment.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
June '40-- no.
This was '39.
This was in '39.
In '39, we [INAUDIBLE] Poland and Germany.
I don't remember the month.
No.
My mother didn't like politics.
But my father was a big politician.
He and a pharmacist, [?
Solis, ?] they used to talk, and the radio to listen.
And he was very good on the map and in geography.
And there was so-- and I picked up from them, you know?
And from-- you know, from [INAUDIBLE] and Madame [?
Peresterova, ?] how they were spies, and they sold out Poland because they were Germans.
And she didn't-- wasn't interested in politics.
But you heard your father talking about it.
Yes.
And if my grandfather came, he was also a politician.
He was a very religious man.
My grandfather write the Torah.
Sofer Torah.
He was a businessman.
This is his hobby.
Four Sefer Torahs he wrote in his life.
Moishe.
Moishe Shteinshneide.
Yes.
And from mother's side, they told me that he was also the same, a sofer.
But this was his-- he make a living from that.
Zayde.
Zayde Baba.
Baba Zayde.
Baba Cyril.
Cyril Shteinshneide.
Yes, yes.
My brother was a big politician also.
In fact, my brother was-- he finished rabbi in the [INAUDIBLE] Yeshiva.
But he didn't want to be a rabbi.
He was the leader from the [INAUDIBLE].
He was very religious, very religious.
He was big, big fan Hasid from Olyka Rebbe.
And he was in Olyka every Saturday.
He used to go to eat with the Rebbe [INAUDIBLE].
No.
The Yeshiva in [INAUDIBLE], he was a rabbi that his father finished the [INAUDIBLE] Yeshiva.
He didn't shave, but he-- with a special machine, you're allowed.
And my father, like Lenin, a beard here, [INAUDIBLE] small.
No payos.
No, my brother also no payos.
But a yarmulke in the head, he had.
No, not, [INAUDIBLE].
No.
Nice-- this suit.
Yes.
Yes.
All the time, of course.
Especially in school.
You know, they were so-- I didn't, because we had workers.
They couldn't be anti-Semitic, because my father would have thrown them out, you know.
They make a living from my father.
And the workers in the forest also.
But when I went to school, they didn't know.
They know, or they didn't know.
To each other, you know, [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what is [NON-ENGLISH].
Yes.
Now I can-- I had a big satisfaction.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The big satisfaction when I came to Israel, and I [? saw ?] the Polacks, how they work, and now they're happy to be in Palestine, Israel, and to make money by the Jews.
Yes.
The [NON-ENGLISH] and the [NON-ENGLISH], two Jewish papers.
No, I read them.
No, I liked-- that time, I liked books.
I read a little bit, but my father used to tell me.
I used to ask him.
He liked to discuss with me.
He liked to discuss.
I was-- he told-- he always told that I-- I have his head, because my brother hate business.
He didn't like business terrible.
The Yeshiva [NON-ENGLISH] didn't like business.
He married a business lady.
And she was in the business, and he helped her out.
And my sister married, and my fourth, Mindel, she-- my mother didn't know even what kind of business my father has.
And I-- I can explain from the farm, and I can explain from the forest, what from which tree you can make out.
Yes.
I used to go with my father.
He used to take me when I had the vacation.
I used to take girlfriends with me too.
And they used to explain.
No, I ask it.
Not only explain, I ask it.
Daddy, ?] why you make from this?
You say this furniture.
He'd say furniture needs to be-- should be this, you know.
And for eating, there must be the [INAUDIBLE] for eating the [INAUDIBLE] wood, you know, for fall out, yes.
And he explained-- explained me, and I know it.
And I know he showed me how to measure, and how to make the material-- everything.
And I liked this.
And when I come I on the farm, then I look at everything.
You know, I took, for instance-- they make, you know, like for all kind-- and come to-- [NON-ENGLISH].
How you say [NON-ENGLISH].
No, when they take off the--
Of the-- [NON-ENGLISH].
Wheat-- wheat-- the produce, yes.
Then I remember everything by the fruit trees, how they make, cultivate the time-- everything.
Because they [INAUDIBLE] trees and they cultivate.
When Hitler gained power, right away--
